examples.title: Truth Table Generator for Compound Proposition
examples.description: Algorithm to generate truth table for any compound proposition with n propositions
examples.code:
ALGORITHM TruthTable(n)
// n = number of propositions
BEGIN
 rows ← 2^n
 FOR i FROM 0 to rows-1 DO
 // Convert i to binary to get truth values
 FOR j FROM 0 to n-1 DO
 prop[j] ← (i >> (n-1-j)) % 2 // 0=FALSE, 1=TRUE
 END FOR
 // Evaluate compound proposition for this row
 result ← EvaluateProposition(prop)
 OUTPUT prop[], result
 END FOR
END

FUNCTION EvaluateProposition(prop)
BEGIN
 // Example: Evaluate p AND (p OR q)
 // p = prop[0], q = prop[1]
 result ← prop[0] AND (prop[0] OR prop[1])
 RETURN result
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode generates all 2^n rows for n propositions. Each row represents a unique combination of truth values. The EvaluateProposition function computes the truth value of the compound proposition for each row.

examples.title: Check for Tautology
examples.description: Algorithm to determine if a compound proposition is a tautology
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CheckTautology(propList, n)
// propList = list of propositions
// n = number of propositions
BEGIN
 rows ← 2^n
 FOR i FROM 0 to rows-1 DO
 truthValues ← GetTruthValues(i, n)
 result ← Evaluate(truthValues)
 IF result = FALSE THEN
 RETURN FALSE // Not a tautology
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN TRUE // All rows resulted in TRUE
END

FUNCTION GetTruthValues(num, n)
BEGIN
 values ← []
 FOR k FROM 0 to n-1 DO
 bit ← (num >> (n-1-k)) % 2
 values.append(bit == 1)
 END FOR
 RETURN values
END
examples.explanation: To check if a proposition is a tautology, we evaluate it for ALL possible truth assignments. If the result is TRUE for every row, it is a tautology. If any row is FALSE, it is not a tautology (could be contradiction or contingency).

examples.title: Evaluate Predicate Logic Formula
examples.description: Algorithm to evaluate a universally quantified predicate formula
examples.explanation: These algorithms simulate quantifier evaluation. ∀ (universal) checks every element - returns TRUE only if ALL satisfy the predicate. ∃ (existential) returns TRUE if AT LEAST ONE satisfies the predicate.
examples.timeComplexity: O(m) for domain of size m

examples.title: Modus Ponens Inference Engine
examples.description: Simple inference system implementing Modus Ponens to derive conclusions
examples.explanation: This algorithm implements automated reasoning using Modus Ponens. Given known implications and confirmed antecedents, it derives the consequent as a new fact. This is fundamental to expert systems and logic programming.
